You foster a culture of speaking up by protecting the first person who does it.
Before we judge the team, we should look into what happened in that meeting.
Let me name the elephant in the room: nobody in this team disagrees with a director.
If leadership had run a pulse survey, they would have seen it coming.
If someone had looked into the exit interviews, they would have found the problem.
They should have addressed it during onboarding.
The two coordinators might not have left if the culture had felt safe.
You have seen the pulse survey. Before the academic team joins us, give me your reading of it.

Two numbers do not agree with each other. Belonging is at seventy eight percent, and psychological safety is at forty one. It would appear that people are proud to be here, and they still do not feel safe enough to disagree.
Some of the directors will tell you the survey is noise. Nine people left last year and every one of them said they were happy.
They said they were happy because the exit interview was the first safe conversation they had. If someone had looked into those nine interviews, we would have had this cultural diagnosis a year ago.
That is a hard thing to say to the people who built this place. How do you plan to say it without losing them?

I will not call it a toxic culture, because it is not one. I will name the elephant in the room: disagreement is expensive here. And then I will ask them what it would take to foster a culture of speaking up.
Before I propose anything, I would like to run a cultural diagnosis.
It would appear that the issue is psychological safety, not belonging.
If someone had looked into the exit interviews, we would have known a year ago.
To some extent, the team might have felt that disagreeing carried a cost.
Let me name the elephant in the room, and you can tell me if I am wrong.

Thank you for making time for this. I have thought a lot about what I would say in an exit interview, and I decided to be honest. I did not leave because of the salary. I left because nobody looked into what was actually happening on my team. We had core values on the wall, and none of them survived the first difficult meeting. If someone had asked me this question a year ago, I would have told you exactly the same thing, and the answer would have cost the company nothing. Instead you waited until I resigned. My onboarding experience was excellent, by the way. Two people welcomed me, the framework was clear, and I was proud to be here. Then the first project went wrong, and the person who spoke up in that meeting was blamed in front of everyone. After that, nobody spoke up again. That is the moment I stopped caring, and I was not the only one.

Danielle, it is Nathan. I have the pulse survey results in front of me, and I want you to see them before we meet. Two numbers stand out. Belonging is at seventy eight percent, which is honestly higher than I expected for an academic team. Psychological safety is at forty one percent. Forty one. So people feel they are part of something, and they still do not feel safe enough to disagree with a senior colleague in a room. Now, here is what I want from you. Do not arrive and tell us we have a toxic culture. We do not, and you would lose the room in thirty seconds. What we have is one specific problem that nobody names out loud, and I would like you to name it. Look into the numbers first, take a stance, and bring me a diagnosis. Not a plan. A diagnosis.
